title: AXCESSR UX Research
text:
AXCESSR is a multidimensional candidate risk assessment tool designed to measure personality, values, and emotional risk factors. Having completed a full psychometric validation pipeline, the next phase of development concerns the candidate-facing experience of the instrument. A technically sound instrument can still yield poor-quality data if the user experience drives disengagement, rushing, or deliberate response manipulation - UX research therefore serves a dual purpose: improving the candidate experience and protecting data integrity.
Because AXCESSR has not yet been deployed in a live environment, clickstream and behavioural interaction data do not yet exist. This section simulates candidate-level UX metrics grounded in real response quality flags from the validation sample, establishing a Version A baseline against which a Version B iteration will be evaluated.

title: AXCESSR UX Research
text:
Version A
Version A establishes the baseline UX profile of the AXCESSR instrument prior to any targeted intervention. This baseline will be used to identify priority intervention points for the Version B UX redesign.
The table below summarises flag prevalence across the full sample (N = 2016), spanning both empirical quality flags and UX engagement metrics.


Show code
n_total <- 2016

tibble(
  `Quality Flag`        = c(
    "Speeder",
    "Low Variance",
    "Long String",
    "Boundary Overuse",
    "Completion (single sitting)",
    "High Click-Through",
    "Early Drop-Off",
    "Uniform RT (Inter-item)",
    "Early Onset of Disengagement",
    "Loading Time",
    "Connectivity Drop"
  ),
  Criterion = c(
    "Total time taken flagged as too short",
    "Person SD < 0.11",
    "Longest string > 15",
    "Proportion of 0s > 0.28 or 1s > 0.50",
    "Did not complete in a single sitting",
    "Click-through rate > 0.70",
    "Abandoned before item 100",
    "RT consistency SD < 1.5",
    "Onset of disengagement before item 60",
    "Average page load time exceeds 5 seconds",
    "One or more connectivity drop events"
  ),
  Count = c(
    sum(ux_version_a$time_flag        == "TRUE", na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$low_var_flag     == "TRUE", na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$longstring_flag  == "TRUE", na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$boundary_flag    == "TRUE", na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$completed_single_sitting == 0, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$click_through_rate > 0.70, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$drop_off_item < 100, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$rt_consistency_sd < 1.5, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$onset_disengagement < 60, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$load_time_seconds > 5.0, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$connectivity_drops > 0, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
) %>%
  mutate(
    Percentage = (Count / n_total * 100) %>% round(2) %>% paste0("%")
  ) %>%
  kable(
    caption = "UX Quality Flag Summary — Version A (N = 2016)",
    align   = c("l", "l", "r", "r")
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
    full_width        = FALSE,
    font_size         = 13
  ) %>%
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, background = "#5b2c6f", color = "white")

Connectivity drop events emerged as the most prevalent flag (18.3%), reflecting the real-world infrastructure constraints of the deployment context. Behavioural disengagement flags - long string, low variance, speeding - remain relatively contained (2–10%), suggesting the majority of the validation sample engaged meaningfully with the instrument. The below plot shows this information visually.


Show code
flag_summary <- tibble(
  flag = c(
    "Speeder",
    "Low Response Variance",
    "Long String",
    "Boundary Overuse",
    "Did Not Complete",
    "High Click-Through",
    "Early Drop-Off",
    "Uniform Response Time",
    "Early Disengagement",
    "Loading Time",
    "Connectivity Drop"
  ),
  count = c(
    sum(ux_version_a$time_flag == 1,                    na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$low_var_flag == 1,                 na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$longstring_flag == 1,              na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$boundary_flag == 1,                na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$completed_single_sitting == 0,     na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$click_through_rate > 0.70,         na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$drop_off_item < 100,               na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$rt_consistency_sd < 1.5,           na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$onset_disengagement < 60,          na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$load_time_seconds > 5.0,           na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$connectivity_drops > 0,            na.rm = TRUE)
  )
) %>%
  mutate(
    percentage = (count / 2016 * 100) %>% round(1),
    flag       = fct_reorder(flag, percentage),
    label      = paste0(percentage, "%")
  )

ggplot(flag_summary, aes(x = percentage, y = flag)) +
  geom_segment(
    aes(x = 0, xend = percentage, y = flag, yend = flag),
    colour = "#9b59b6", linewidth = 0.8
  ) +
  geom_point(colour = "#5b2c6f", size = 4) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = label),
    hjust = -0.3, size = 3.5, colour = "grey30"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    limits = c(0, max(flag_summary$percentage) * 1.2),
    labels = scales::label_percent(scale = 1)
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "UX Quality Flag Prevalence - Version A",
    subtitle = "Percentage of candidates (N = 2016) flagged on each metric",
    x        = "Percentage Flagged",
    y        = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(colour = "grey50", size = 11),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank()
  )










Diagnostic summary
The flag prevalence data surfaces four priority concerns heading into Version B. Completion rate is the headline issue - nearly 13% of candidates did not finish in a single sitting, and a further 18% experienced at least one connectivity drop event, suggesting that session length is creating meaningful exposure to both fatigue and infrastructure risk. Boundary overuse (9.52%), high click-through rate (10.81%), and speeders (9.97%) point to a secondary problem: a non-trivial minority of candidates are not engaging authentically with item content, whether through rushing or systematic extreme responding.
These patterns are not independent. A long instrument increases fatigue, fatigue drives disengagement, and disengagement manifests as the flag co-occurrences visible in the high-flag group. The data therefore points to a single primary lever: reducing instrument length through adaptive delivery.


Recommendations

Adaptive Item Delivery via CAT

The most impactful single change. A Computerised Adaptive Testing engine reduces the number of items administered per candidate by selecting only the most informative items given each candidate’s response pattern. This directly addresses completion rate, connectivity exposure, click-through rate, and onset of disengagement — all of which are downstream consequences of instrument length. A shorter, targeted assessment reduces fatigue, limits connectivity risk, and removes the motivation to rush.

Platform Load Optimisation

With 10.66% of candidates experiencing load times exceeding 5 seconds and 18.3% recording at least one connectivity drop event, platform performance represents a meaningful UX risk independent of candidate behaviour. Version B should simulate the effect of optimised asset delivery and offline-capable session caching — particularly relevant given variable connectivity in the deployment context.

In-Session Engagement Support

Progress indicators, estimated time remaining, and structured section breaks are low-cost UX interventions with well-documented effects on sustained engagement. These target the onset of disengagement metric specifically — pushing the point at which candidates begin straight-lining or boundary responding later into the assessment, or eliminating it altogether for borderline candidates.

title: AXCESSR UX Research
text:
Time
The most immediate consequence of CAT-based adaptive delivery is a compression of completion times. Version A shows the characteristic right-skewed distribution of a fixed-form instrument — a healthy central mass with a long tail driven by connectivity issues, session abandonment, and disengagement. Version B simulates the expected shift under adaptive delivery: a tighter, more normally distributed profile centred around a shorter mean, with both extremes reduced. The disappearance of the extreme right tail reflects fewer candidates experiencing prolonged sessions, while the left tail contraction reflects the adjusted speeder threshold calibrated to the shorter instrument.


Show code
# Time comparison - histograms

bind_rows(
  ux_version_a %>% mutate(Version = "Version A"),
  ux_version_b %>% mutate(Version = "Version B")
) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = time_taken_seconds, fill = Version, colour = Version)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, alpha = 0.85) +
  facet_wrap(~ Version, ncol = 2, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Version A" = "#3B1F5E",
    "Version B" = "#C4A8E8"
  )) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(
    "Version A" = "#3B1F5E",
    "Version B" = "#9B70CC"
  )) +
  labs(
    title    = "Completion Time: Version A vs Version B",
    subtitle = "Distribution of time taken (seconds) across both instrument versions",
    x        = "Time Taken (seconds)",
    y        = "Number of Respondents",
    fill     = NULL, colour = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#1A1A1A"),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(colour = "#888888", size = 9),
    strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#3B1F5E"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    legend.position  = "none",
    plot.background  = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = NA)
  )

title: AXCESSR UX Research
text:
Person-Level Variance
Response variance tells a subtler story than time. Where time taken captures behavioural pace, variance captures response authenticity - whether candidates were genuinely differentiating between items or responding indiscriminately. Version B simulates the expected improvement in variance distribution following engagement support interventions: the left tail of near-zero responders shifts rightward, reflecting fewer candidates straight-lining through a shorter, better-paced instrument. The healthy central distribution remains largely intact, as the majority of Version A candidates were already responding meaningfully.


Show code
# Response Variance comparison - histograms

bind_rows(
  ux_version_a %>% mutate(Version = "Version A"),
  ux_version_b %>% mutate(Version = "Version B")
) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = person_sd, fill = Version, colour = Version)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, alpha = 0.85) +
  facet_wrap(~ Version, ncol = 2, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Version A" = "#3B1F5E",
    "Version B" = "#C4A8E8"
  )) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(
    "Version A" = "#3B1F5E",
    "Version B" = "#9B70CC"
  )) +
  labs(
    title    = "Person-Level Variance: Version A vs Version B",
    subtitle = "Distribution of response variance across both instrument versions",
    x        = "Person-Level Variance",
    y        = "Number of Respondents",
    fill     = NULL, colour = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#1A1A1A"),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(colour = "#888888", size = 9),
    strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#3B1F5E"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    legend.position  = "none",
    plot.background  = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = NA)
  )

title: AXCESSR UX Research
text:
Density Overlaps
Four continuous UX metrics are compared below as density overlays, allowing direct visual comparison of distributional shift between versions. Click-through rate should shift leftward in Version B - fewer candidates rushing. RT consistency SD should shift rightward - more natural variation in pacing. Load time should compress toward zero following platform optimisation. Onset of disengagement should shift rightward and thin out, reflecting fewer candidates hitting a disengagement point and those who do reaching it later in the session.


Show code
# Density Overlay plots

bind_rows(
  ux_version_a %>% mutate(Version = "Version A"),
  ux_version_b %>% mutate(Version = "Version B")
) %>%
  select(
    Version,
    `Click-Through Rate`       = click_through_rate,
    `RT Consistency SD`        = rt_consistency_sd,
    `Load Time (seconds)`      = load_time_seconds,
    `Onset of Disengagement`   = onset_disengagement
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    -Version,
    names_to  = "Metric",
    values_to = "Value"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Metric = factor(Metric, levels = c(
      "Click-Through Rate",
      "RT Consistency SD",
      "Load Time (seconds)",
      "Onset of Disengagement"
    ))
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Value, fill = Version, colour = Version)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.35, linewidth = 0.7, na.rm = TRUE) +
  facet_wrap(~ Metric, scales = "free", ncol = 2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Version A" = "#3B1F5E",
    "Version B" = "#C4A8E8"
  )) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(
    "Version A" = "#3B1F5E",
    "Version B" = "#9B70CC"
  )) +
  labs(
    title    = "UX Metric Distributions: Version A vs Version B",
    subtitle = "Density overlays across four continuous engagement metrics",
    x        = "Value",
    y        = "Density",
    fill     = NULL, colour = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#1A1A1A"),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(colour = "#888888", size = 9),
    strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#3B1F5E"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    legend.position  = "top",
    plot.background  = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = NA)
  )

title: AXCESSR UX Research
text:
UX Metric Shift Plot
The dumbbell chart below provides a single consolidated view of improvement across all eleven UX metrics, comparing flag prevalence between Version A and Version B. Each flag is shown as a connected pair — the dark point representing Version A prevalence and the light point representing Version B. Metrics where the gap between points is largest represent the greatest UX gains from the three interventions. Metrics with little movement reflect either dispositional factors that UX changes cannot address, or infrastructure constraints that require solutions beyond instrument redesign.


Show code
# Dumbbell plot comparison

n_total <- 2016

flag_a <- tibble(
  flag = c(
    "Speeder", "Low Variance", "Long String", "Boundary Overuse",
    "Did Not Complete", "High Click-Through", "Early Drop-Off",
    "Uniform RT", "Early Disengagement", "Loading Time", "Connectivity Drop"
  ),
  pct_a = c(
    sum(ux_version_a$time_flag == 1,                  na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$low_var_flag == 1,               na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$longstring_flag == 1,            na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$boundary_flag == 1,              na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$completed_single_sitting == 0,   na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$click_through_rate > 0.70,       na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$drop_off_item < 100,             na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$rt_consistency_sd < 1.5,         na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$onset_disengagement < 60,        na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$load_time_seconds > 5.0,         na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$connectivity_drops > 0,          na.rm = TRUE)
  ) / n_total * 100
)

flag_b <- tibble(
  flag = c(
    "Speeder", "Low Variance", "Long String", "Boundary Overuse",
    "Did Not Complete", "High Click-Through", "Early Drop-Off",
    "Uniform RT", "Early Disengagement", "Loading Time", "Connectivity Drop"
  ),
  pct_b = c(
    sum(ux_version_b$time_flag == 1,                  na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_b$low_var_flag == 1,               na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_b$longstring_flag == 1,            na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_b$boundary_flag == 1,              na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_b$completed_single_sitting == 0,   na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_b$click_through_rate > 0.70,       na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_b$drop_off_item < 100,             na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_b$rt_consistency_sd < 1.5,         na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_b$onset_disengagement < 60,        na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_b$load_time_seconds > 5.0,         na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_b$connectivity_drops > 0,          na.rm = TRUE)
  ) / n_total * 100
)

dumbbell_data <- left_join(flag_a, flag_b, by = "flag") %>%
  mutate(flag = fct_reorder(flag, pct_a))

ggplot(dumbbell_data) +
  geom_segment(
    aes(x = pct_b, xend = pct_a, y = flag, yend = flag),
    colour = "grey70", linewidth = 1.2
  ) +
  geom_point(aes(x = pct_a, y = flag), 
             colour = "#3B1F5E", size = 4) +
  geom_point(aes(x = pct_b, y = flag), 
             colour = "#C4A8E8", size = 4) +
  geom_text(aes(x = pct_a, y = flag, 
                label = paste0(round(pct_a, 1), "%")),
            hjust = -0.4, size = 3.2, colour = "#3B1F5E") +
  geom_text(aes(x = pct_b, y = flag,
                label = paste0(round(pct_b, 1), "%")),
            hjust =  1.4, size = 3.2, colour = "#9B70CC") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    limits = c(-2, max(dumbbell_data$pct_a) * 1.25),
    labels = scales::label_percent(scale = 1)
  ) +
  annotate("point", x = 19, y = 3, colour = "#3B1F5E", size = 4) +
  annotate("text",  x = 20, y = 3, label = "Version A", 
           hjust = 0, size = 3.2, colour = "#3B1F5E") +
  annotate("point", x = 19, y = 2, colour = "#C4A8E8", size = 4) +
  annotate("text",  x = 20, y = 2, label = "Version B", 
           hjust = 0, size = 3.2, colour = "#9B70CC") +
  labs(
    title    = "UX Flag Prevalence: Version A vs Version B",
    subtitle = "Percentage of candidates flagged on each metric — dark = Version A, light = Version B",
    x        = "Percentage Flagged",
    y        = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title         = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#1A1A1A"),
    plot.subtitle      = element_text(colour = "#888888", size = 9),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    plot.background    = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = NA)
  )

title: AXCESSR Data Validity
text:
AXCESSR is a multidimensional candidate risk assessment tool designed to measure personality, values, and emotional risk factors. This section documents the psychometric evaluation of the AXCESSR item pool and the evidence-based item refinement process undertaken prior to scoring. The goal is not to reproduce the full structural model - which constitutes proprietary instrument design - but to demonstrate that the retained item set meets accepted standards for reliability, internal consistency, and discriminant validity.


Show code
# Reading in data
validity_data <- read_excel(here("data/AXCESSR_validity_data_isolated - 2026.04.24.xlsx"))

title: AXCESSR Data Validity
section: Score Distributions Before and After Removal
text:
Score Distributions Before and After Removal
The plots below show the distribution of domain-level scores before and after item removal. Improved item quality is expected to yield tighter, better-differentiated distributions with reduced floor and ceiling effects.


Show code
# Score Comparison Table
comparison <- validity_data %>%
  select(id,
         # Original scores
         personality_risk, values_risk,
         emotion_traits_risk, emotion_states_risk,
         total_risk_weighted,
             # Revised scores
             personality_risk_check, values_risk_check,
             emotion_traits_risk_check, emotion_states_risk_check,
             total_risk_weighted_check) %>% 
  mutate(across(everything(), as.numeric))

# Mean comparison table


comparison_table <- tibble(
  Score = c(
    "Personality Risk", "Values Risk",
    "Emotion Traits Risk", "Emotion States Risk",
    "Total Risk (Weighted)"
  ),
  Original = c(
    mean(comparison$personality_risk,        na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(comparison$values_risk,             na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(comparison$emotion_traits_risk,     na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(comparison$emotion_states_risk,     na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(comparison$total_risk_weighted,     na.rm = TRUE)
  ),
  Revised = c(
    mean(comparison$personality_risk_check,        na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(comparison$values_risk_check,             na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(comparison$emotion_traits_risk_check,     na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(comparison$emotion_states_risk_check,     na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(comparison$total_risk_weighted_check,     na.rm = TRUE)
  )
) %>%
  mutate(
    Difference  = round(Revised - Original, 4),
    Original    = round(Original, 4),
    Revised     = round(Revised, 4)
  )

kbl(comparison_table,
             col.names = c("Score", "Original", "Revised", 
                           "Difference"),
             caption   = "Mean Score Comparison: Original vs Revised") %>% 
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
    full_width = TRUE,
    font_size = 14
  )



Mean Score Comparison: Original vs Revised

title: AXCESSR Data Validity
text:
PSI
To assess whether item removal materially altered candidate risk profiles, the Population Stability Index (PSI) was computed between original (V1) and revised (V2) scores at the total and domain level. PSI quantifies the distributional shift between two scoring populations and is widely used in credit risk modelling as a model monitoring metric. Scores below 0.10 indicate a stable distribution; values between 0.10 and 0.25 indicate moderate shift warranting investigation; values above 0.25 indicate significant drift requiring model review.


Show code
# PSI scores across key domains

# Total weighted score PSI
psi_total <- psi(
  base   = comparison$total_risk_weighted,
  target = comparison$total_risk_weighted_check,
  bin    = 5,
  alpha  = 0.05
)

# Domain-level PSI
psi_personality <- psi(
  base   = comparison$personality_risk,
  target = comparison$personality_risk_check,
  bin    = 5, alpha = 0.05
)

psi_values <- psi(
  base   = comparison$values_risk,
  target = comparison$values_risk_check,
  bin    = 5, alpha = 0.05
)

psi_emotion_traits <- psi(
  base   = comparison$emotion_traits_risk,
  target = comparison$emotion_traits_risk_check,
  bin    = 5, alpha = 0.05
)

psi_emotion_states <- psi(
  base   = comparison$emotion_states_risk,
  target = comparison$emotion_states_risk_check,
  bin    = 5, alpha = 0.05
)

# Pull just the PSI values cleanly
psi_table <- tibble(
  Score = c("Total Risk (Weighted)", "Personality Risk", 
            "Values Risk", "Emotion Traits Risk", "Emotion States Risk"),
  PSI = c(
    psi_total$res$psi,
    psi_personality$res$psi,
    psi_values$res$psi,
    psi_emotion_traits$res$psi,
    psi_emotion_states$res$psi
  ),
  Interpretation = c(
    "Stable - minor shift, no action required",
    "Stable - distribution largely unchanged",
    "Moderate shift - high considering only 9 items were removed",
    "Highly stable - negligible distributional change",
    "Stable - minimal shift across versions"
  )
)

kbl(
  psi_table,
  col.names = c("Score", "PSI", "Interpretation"),
  caption   = "Population Stability Index (PSI) Results"
) %>% 
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
    full_width = TRUE,
    font_size = 14
  )



Population Stability Index (PSI) Results


Score
PSI
Interpretation




Total Risk (Weighted)
0.0997377
Stable - minor shift, no action required


Personality Risk
0.0283380
Stable - distribution largely unchanged


Values Risk
0.1884240
Moderate shift - high considering only 9 items were removed


Emotion Traits Risk
0.0036229
Highly stable - negligible distributional change


Emotion States Risk
0.0420474
Stable - minimal shift across versions





Overall, the revised instrument demonstrated strong score stability. The total weighted risk score produced a PSI of 0.10, sitting at the boundary between stable and moderate shift — indicating that item removal had minimal practical impact on how candidates are ranked and classified overall. Emotion Traits Risk (PSI = 0.004) and Emotion States Risk (PSI = 0.042) were the most stable domains, suggesting that structural refinements in these domains preserved the underlying score distributions almost entirely.
Personality Risk showed a small but acceptable shift (PSI = 0.028), well within the stable range. Values Risk showed the largest shift of the four domains (PSI = 0.188), falling in the moderate range. This is notable given that the Values domain underwent meaningful structural consolidation — the distributional shift reflects genuine refinement of the construct rather than model instability, and remains well below the 0.25 threshold that would require intervention.
Taken together, the PSI results confirm that the revised instrument produces risk classifications that are substantively consistent with the original, while reflecting targeted improvements to construct validity at the domain level.


Show code
# PSI Bar Chart
psi_df <- tibble(
  Domain = c("Emotion Traits", "Personality", "Emotion States",
             "Total Risk\n(Weighted)", "Values"),
  PSI    = c(0.0036, 0.0283, 0.0420, 0.0997, 0.1884),
  Zone   = case_when(
    PSI < 0.10 ~ "Stable",
    PSI < 0.25 ~ "Moderate",
    TRUE       ~ "Intervention Required"
  )
) %>%
  mutate(Domain = fct_reorder(Domain, PSI))

ggplot(psi_df, aes(x = PSI, y = Domain, fill = Zone)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.55) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.10, linetype = "dashed",
             colour = "#6B3FA0", linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.25, linetype = "dashed",
             colour = "#C0392B", linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(PSI, 3)),
            hjust = -0.2, size = 3.5, colour = "#1A1A1A") +
  annotate("text", x = 0.06, y = 0.6, label = "Stable threshold",
           hjust = 0, size = 3, colour = "#6B3FA0") +
  annotate("text", x = 0.2, y = 0.6, label = "Intervention threshold",
           hjust = 0, size = 3, colour = "#C0392B") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Stable"    = "#3B1F5E",
    "Moderate"  = "#E8A838"
  )) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 0.28), expand = c(0, 0)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Population Stability Index (PSI) by Domain",
    subtitle = "V1 (247 items) vs V2 (206 items)",
    x        = "PSI",
    y        = NULL,
    fill     = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#1A1A1A"),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(colour = "#888888", size = 10),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    legend.position  = "top",
    plot.background  = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = NA)
  )  









Show code
ggsave("image/psi_bar.png", width = 12, height = 6, dpi = 180, bg = "white")


The bar chart confirms that distributional shift is concentrated in the Values domain. All other domains sit comfortably below the stable threshold, and the total weighted risk score sits precisely at the boundary (PSI = 0.10) - indicating that at the overall scoring level, V1 and V2 produce near-equivalent candidate classifications.


Show code
# Density Overlays 

score_long <- comparison %>%
  select(
    `Total Risk\n(Weighted)` = total_risk_weighted,
    `Total Risk\n(Weighted) V2` = total_risk_weighted_check,
    Personality = personality_risk,
    `Personality V2` = personality_risk_check,
    Values = values_risk,
    `Values V2` = values_risk_check,
    `Emotion Traits` = emotion_traits_risk,
    `Emotion Traits V2` = emotion_traits_risk_check,
    `Emotion States` = emotion_states_risk,
    `Emotion States V2` = emotion_states_risk_check
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "Domain", values_to = "Score") %>%
  mutate(
    Version = if_else(str_detect(Domain, "V2"), "V2 (206 items)", "V1 (247 items)"),
    Domain  = str_remove(Domain, " V2"),
    Domain  = factor(
      Domain,
      levels = c(
        "Total Risk\n(Weighted)",
        "Personality",
        "Values",
        "Emotion Traits",
        "Emotion States"
      )
    )
  )

ggplot(score_long, aes(x = Score, fill = Version, colour = Version)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.35, linewidth = 0.7) +
  facet_wrap(~ Domain, scales = "free_y", ncol = 3) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "V1 (247 items)" = "#C4A8E8",
    "V2 (206 items)" = "#3B1F5E"
  )) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(
    "V1 (247 items)" = "#9B70CC",
    "V2 (206 items)" = "#3B1F5E"
  )) +
  labs(
    title    = "Score Distributions: V1 vs V2",
    subtitle = "Density curves by domain",
    x        = "Risk Score",
    y        = "Density",
    fill     = NULL, colour = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title         = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#1A1A1A"),
    plot.subtitle      = element_text(colour = "#888888", size = 9),
    strip.text         = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#3B1F5E"),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    legend.position    = "top",
    plot.background    = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = NA)
  )









Show code
ggsave("image/psi_density.png", width = 10, height = 6, dpi = 180, bg = "white")


The density curves corroborate the PSI findings. For Emotion Traits and Personality, the V1 and V2 distributions are almost indistinguishable. Emotion States and Total Risk show a slight tightening of the distribution in V2 - consistent with the removal of noisy items reducing score variance. The Values domain shows the most visible shift, with the V2 distribution marginally narrower and shifted slightly rightward, reflecting the influence of the removed items on the lower end of the score range. Across all domains, the core shape of the distribution is preserved, confirming that the revised instrument measures the same underlying constructs in a substantively equivalent way.

title: AXCESSR Data Validity
text:
Structural Recommendations
Item analysis identified several subfactors with overlapping variance structures, supporting consolidation of theoretically adjacent constructs. The following structural revisions are recommended prior to finalising the scoring model.


Show code
# Structural Recommendations

structural_table <- tibble::tribble(
  ~Original,                                      ~Recommended,                        ~Rationale,
  "Stress Tolerance, Flexibility, Optimism",      "Collapse \u2192 Adjustment",    "High inter-subfactor correlations; indistinct factor profiles",
    "Dependence",                                   "Flag for item redevelopment or removal",   "Cross-loaded across factors",
  "Compulsivity, Impulsivity and Boredom",                        "Collapse \u2192 Dysregulation", "Shared item content bar some Impulsivity items",
  "Control, Fearfulness, Anxiety",                "Collapse \u2192 Affectivity",   "Empirically inseparable; theoretically coherent at factor level"
)

structural_table %>% 
  kbl(
    col.names = c("Original Subfactors", "Recommended Structure", "Rationale"),
    align = c("l", "l", "l"),
    escape = FALSE
  ) %>% 
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
    full_width = TRUE,
    font_size = 14
  ) %>% 
  row_spec(0, bold = FALSE) %>% 
  column_spec(1, width = "30%") %>% 
  column_spec(2, width = "25%") %>% 
  column_spec(3, width = "45%")

title: AXCESSR Data Validity
text:
Score Distribution Comparison
The plots below show the distribution of domain-level scores before and after item removal. Improved item quality is expected to yield tighter, better-differentiated distributions with reduced floor and ceiling effects.


Show code
# Score Comparison Table

comparison <- validity_data %>%
  select(id,
         # Original scores
         personality_risk, values_risk,
         emotion_traits_risk, emotion_states_risk,
         total_risk_weighted,
             # Revised scores
             personality_risk_check, values_risk_check,
             emotion_traits_risk_check, emotion_states_risk_check,
             total_risk_weighted_check) %>% 
  mutate(across(everything(), as.numeric))

# Mean comparison table

comparison_table <- tibble(
  Score = c(
    "Personality Risk", "Values Risk",
    "Emotion Traits Risk", "Emotion States Risk",
    "Total Risk (Weighted)"
  ),
  Original = c(
    mean(comparison$personality_risk,        na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(comparison$values_risk,             na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(comparison$emotion_traits_risk,     na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(comparison$emotion_states_risk,     na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(comparison$total_risk_weighted,     na.rm = TRUE)
  ),
  Revised = c(
    mean(comparison$personality_risk_check,        na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(comparison$values_risk_check,             na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(comparison$emotion_traits_risk_check,     na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(comparison$emotion_states_risk_check,     na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(comparison$total_risk_weighted_check,     na.rm = TRUE)
  )
) %>%
  mutate(
    Difference  = round(Revised - Original, 4),
    Original    = round(Original, 4),
    Revised     = round(Revised, 4)
  )

kbl(comparison_table,
             col.names = c("Score", "Original", "Revised", 
                           "Difference"),
             caption   = "Mean Score Comparison: Original vs Revised") %>% 
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
    full_width = TRUE,
    font_size = 14
  )

title: AXCESSR Data Validity
text:
Reliability Comparison
Cronbach’s α was averaged across subfactors within each domain and compared between the original (V1, 247 items) and revised (V2, 206 items) instruments. Three of the four domains showed improvement following item removal, with Personality and Values showing the most meaningful gains. Emotion States improved marginally, and Emotion Traits remained stable - suggesting the removed items were contributing little noise in that domain to begin with. Taken together, these findings indicate that targeted item removal had its intended effect: a modest but consistent improvement in internal consistency without destabilising the existing structure.


Show code
# Domain Reliability Comparison

knitr::include_graphics(here::here("image/reliability_domain_dotplot.png"))








Figure 2

title: AXCESSR Conceptualisation
text:
Purpose of the Assessment
The primary objectives of AXCESSR are to:

Improve access to student funding for academically eligible candidates without an established credit or academic record
Match students to programmes where they are likely to succeed
Provide higher education institutions with completion-likelihood signals beyond academic records
Provide lenders with repayment-behaviour predictors validated for thin-file populations
Support risk assessment, underwriting, and portfolio management for funders

title: AXCESSR Conceptualisation
text:
How AXCESSR was built
AXCESSR was not built with data science as an afterthought. From initial construct mapping and conceptualisation through to data validity and candidate screening, and into UX research, every stage of development was data-informed. The pipeline below illustrates how each phase fed into the next.
AXCESSR Development Pipeline

The pipeline above illustrates the three core development phases of AXCESSR - conceptualisation, validation, and UX research - each producing data that feeds the next, and each iteration of the cycle informing a sharper version of the instrument. This is not a linear build. AXCESSR is designed to evolve: findings from validation reshape the item pool, UX research exposes where the assessment places unnecessary burden, and both inform the decisions that define the next iteration - including the move toward a Computerised Adaptive Testing (CAT) engine that shortens assessment time and reduces respondent load without sacrificing measurement precision.
The first cycle began with construct mapping and item generation, grounded in established psychometric reference scales. Validation followed - reliability analyses, IRT diagnostics, and the removal of 41 problematic items refined the instrument from 247 to 206 items. UX research then surfaced the real-world experience of respondents, closing the loop and opening the next one. Each phase fed the next, with all phases being led by data.

title: AXCESSR Conceptualisation
text:
The problem
AXCESSR is a psychometric-based credit risk assessment platform designed to extend financial access to individuals overlooked by traditional credit origination methods - either due to limited credit history or an overestimation of risk based on conventional metrics. Rather than relying on historical financial data, the platform evaluates creditworthiness through the measurement of stable personal dispositions shown to predict financial decision-making and repayment behaviour.
The Funding Access Gap

The platform targets two primary markets: the student loan market, where millions of academically eligible students lack access to funding, and the broader personal credit market, where thin-file and credit-invisible individuals remain underserved. Psychometric scores can be generated for any candidate irrespective of formal credit history and, because scores are user-generated, they are broadly compliant with consumer privacy regulations.

title: AXCESSR Conceptualisation
text:
Assessment Architecture
Four core psychological domains were identified through a review of the behavioural science literature as the constructs most predictive of real-world financial and educational outcomes:

Personality — stable trait dispositions that shape how individuals behave across contexts
Values — motivational orientations that drive long-term decision-making and goal alignment
Emotional Traits — enduring patterns of emotional responding, including impulsivity and self-regulation
Emotional States — situational affective tendencies such as stress reactivity and boredom proneness

AXCESSR Structure

With the domain structure established theoretically, the next challenge was operationalisation - translating abstract constructs into measurable, defensible items

title: AXCESSR Conceptualisation
text:
Item Development
Item development drew on a structured review of established psychometric frameworks, adapting content from validated instruments across each risk domain. Source instruments included the International Personality Item Pool (IPIP), the HEXACO Personality Inventory, the Schwartz Value Survey (SVS), the Trait Emotional Intelligence Questionnaire (TEIQue), the Short Dark Triad (SD3), the Edwards Compulsive Buying Scale (ECBS), the Barratt Impulsivity Scale, the Sensation Seeking Scale, and the UPPS Impulsive Behaviour Scale.
The below figure illustrates the diverse psychological frameworks and reputable assessment scales that were integrated to develop the AXCESSR item pool, highlighting the foundational instruments that informed its construction
Frameworks Underpinning the AXCESSR Item Pool Development

Every item in the AXCESSR pool traces back to a documented psychological construct. Items were not written opportunistically - each one was derived from a defined theoretical basis and subjected to a structured development pipeline before inclusion in the instrument.
Item Development Structure

Each item was engineered to measure a specific construct dimension with precision. The example below illustrates the structural components of a single AXCESSR.
Item Anatomy







Component
Example




Construct
Impulse regulation under financial stress


Item stem
“When I am under pressure, I tend to make decisions quickly without thinking them through.”


Response format
Continuous slider — strongly disagree to strongly agree


Scoring
Continuous 0–1, reverse-scored, feeds Emotion Traits domain


Risk direction
Higher score = elevated impulsivity risk

title: AXCESSR Conceptualisation
text:
From Score to Decision
Item responses aggregate upward through the hierarchy, producing an actionable output at each level.
Item responses → Subfactor score → Factor score → Domain score → Risk profile

title: AXCESSR UX Research
text:
Time
The most immediate consequence of CAT-based adaptive delivery is a compression of completion times. Version A shows the characteristic right-skewed distribution of a fixed-form instrument — a healthy central mass with a long tail driven by connectivity issues, session abandonment, and disengagement. Version B simulates the expected shift under adaptive delivery: a tighter, more normally distributed profile centred around a shorter mean, with both extremes reduced. The disappearance of the extreme right tail reflects fewer candidates experiencing prolonged sessions, while the left tail contraction reflects the adjusted speeder threshold calibrated to the shorter instrument.


Show code
# Time comparison - histograms

bind_rows(
  ux_version_a %>% mutate(Version = "Version A"),
  ux_version_b %>% mutate(Version = "Version B")
) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = time_taken_seconds, fill = Version, colour = Version)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, alpha = 0.85) +
  facet_wrap(~ Version, ncol = 2, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Version A" = "#3B1F5E",
    "Version B" = "#C4A8E8"
  )) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(
    "Version A" = "#3B1F5E",
    "Version B" = "#9B70CC"
  )) +
  labs(
    title    = "Completion Time: Version A vs Version B",
    subtitle = "Distribution of time taken (seconds) across both instrument versions",
    x        = "Time Taken (seconds)",
    y        = "Number of Respondents",
    fill     = NULL, colour = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#1A1A1A"),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(colour = "#888888", size = 9),
    strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#3B1F5E"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    legend.position  = "none",
    plot.background  = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = NA)
  )

title: AXCESSR UX Research
text:
Person-Level Variance
Response variance tells a subtler story than time. Where time taken captures behavioural pace, variance captures response authenticity - whether candidates were genuinely differentiating between items or responding indiscriminately. Version B simulates the expected improvement in variance distribution following engagement support interventions: the left tail of near-zero responders shifts rightward, reflecting fewer candidates straight-lining through a shorter, better-paced instrument. The healthy central distribution remains largely intact, as the majority of Version A candidates were already responding meaningfully.


Show code
# Response Variance comparison - histograms

bind_rows(
  ux_version_a %>% mutate(Version = "Version A"),
  ux_version_b %>% mutate(Version = "Version B")
) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = person_sd, fill = Version, colour = Version)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, alpha = 0.85) +
  facet_wrap(~ Version, ncol = 2, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Version A" = "#3B1F5E",
    "Version B" = "#C4A8E8"
  )) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(
    "Version A" = "#3B1F5E",
    "Version B" = "#9B70CC"
  )) +
  labs(
    title    = "Person-Level Variance: Version A vs Version B",
    subtitle = "Distribution of response variance across both instrument versions",
    x        = "Person-Level Variance",
    y        = "Number of Respondents",
    fill     = NULL, colour = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#1A1A1A"),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(colour = "#888888", size = 9),
    strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#3B1F5E"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    legend.position  = "none",
    plot.background  = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = NA)
  )

title: AXCESSR UX Research
text:
Density Overlaps
Four continuous UX metrics are compared below as density overlays, allowing direct visual comparison of distributional shift between versions. Click-through rate should shift leftward in Version B - fewer candidates rushing. RT consistency SD should shift rightward - more natural variation in pacing. Load time should compress toward zero following platform optimisation. Onset of disengagement should shift rightward and thin out, reflecting fewer candidates hitting a disengagement point and those who do reaching it later in the session.


Show code
# Density Overlay plots

bind_rows(
  ux_version_a %>% mutate(Version = "Version A"),
  ux_version_b %>% mutate(Version = "Version B")
) %>%
  select(
    Version,
    `Click-Through Rate`       = click_through_rate,
    `RT Consistency SD`        = rt_consistency_sd,
    `Load Time (seconds)`      = load_time_seconds,
    `Onset of Disengagement`   = onset_disengagement
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    -Version,
    names_to  = "Metric",
    values_to = "Value"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Metric = factor(Metric, levels = c(
      "Click-Through Rate",
      "RT Consistency SD",
      "Load Time (seconds)",
      "Onset of Disengagement"
    ))
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Value, fill = Version, colour = Version)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.35, linewidth = 0.7, na.rm = TRUE) +
  facet_wrap(~ Metric, scales = "free", ncol = 2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Version A" = "#3B1F5E",
    "Version B" = "#C4A8E8"
  )) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(
    "Version A" = "#3B1F5E",
    "Version B" = "#9B70CC"
  )) +
  labs(
    title    = "UX Metric Distributions: Version A vs Version B",
    subtitle = "Density overlays across four continuous engagement metrics",
    x        = "Value",
    y        = "Density",
    fill     = NULL, colour = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#1A1A1A"),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(colour = "#888888", size = 9),
    strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#3B1F5E"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    legend.position  = "top",
    plot.background  = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = NA)
  )

title: AXCESSR UX Research
text:
UX Metric Shift Plot
The dumbbell chart below provides a single consolidated view of improvement across all eleven UX metrics, comparing flag prevalence between Version A and Version B. Each flag is shown as a connected pair — the dark point representing Version A prevalence and the light point representing Version B. Metrics where the gap between points is largest represent the greatest UX gains from the three interventions. Metrics with little movement reflect either dispositional factors that UX changes cannot address, or infrastructure constraints that require solutions beyond instrument redesign.


Show code
# Dumbbell plot comparison

n_total <- 2016

flag_a <- tibble(
  flag = c(
    "Speeder", "Low Variance", "Long String", "Boundary Overuse",
    "Did Not Complete", "High Click-Through", "Early Drop-Off",
    "Uniform RT", "Early Disengagement", "Loading Time", "Connectivity Drop"
  ),
  pct_a = c(
    sum(ux_version_a$time_flag == 1,                  na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$low_var_flag == 1,               na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$longstring_flag == 1,            na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$boundary_flag == 1,              na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$completed_single_sitting == 0,   na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$click_through_rate > 0.70,       na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$drop_off_item < 100,             na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$rt_consistency_sd < 1.5,         na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$onset_disengagement < 60,        na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$load_time_seconds > 5.0,         na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_a$connectivity_drops > 0,          na.rm = TRUE)
  ) / n_total * 100
)

flag_b <- tibble(
  flag = c(
    "Speeder", "Low Variance", "Long String", "Boundary Overuse",
    "Did Not Complete", "High Click-Through", "Early Drop-Off",
    "Uniform RT", "Early Disengagement", "Loading Time", "Connectivity Drop"
  ),
  pct_b = c(
    sum(ux_version_b$time_flag == 1,                  na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_b$low_var_flag == 1,               na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_b$longstring_flag == 1,            na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_b$boundary_flag == 1,              na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_b$completed_single_sitting == 0,   na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_b$click_through_rate > 0.70,       na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_b$drop_off_item < 100,             na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_b$rt_consistency_sd < 1.5,         na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_b$onset_disengagement < 60,        na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_b$load_time_seconds > 5.0,         na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(ux_version_b$connectivity_drops > 0,          na.rm = TRUE)
  ) / n_total * 100
)

dumbbell_data <- left_join(flag_a, flag_b, by = "flag") %>%
  mutate(flag = fct_reorder(flag, pct_a))

ggplot(dumbbell_data) +
  geom_segment(
    aes(x = pct_b, xend = pct_a, y = flag, yend = flag),
    colour = "grey70", linewidth = 1.2
  ) +
  geom_point(aes(x = pct_a, y = flag), 
             colour = "#3B1F5E", size = 4) +
  geom_point(aes(x = pct_b, y = flag), 
             colour = "#C4A8E8", size = 4) +
  geom_text(aes(x = pct_a, y = flag, 
                label = paste0(round(pct_a, 1), "%")),
            hjust = -0.4, size = 3.2, colour = "#3B1F5E") +
  geom_text(aes(x = pct_b, y = flag,
                label = paste0(round(pct_b, 1), "%")),
            hjust =  1.4, size = 3.2, colour = "#9B70CC") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    limits = c(-2, max(dumbbell_data$pct_a) * 1.25),
    labels = scales::label_percent(scale = 1)
  ) +
  annotate("point", x = 19, y = 3, colour = "#3B1F5E", size = 4) +
  annotate("text",  x = 20, y = 3, label = "Version A", 
           hjust = 0, size = 3.2, colour = "#3B1F5E") +
  annotate("point", x = 19, y = 2, colour = "#C4A8E8", size = 4) +
  annotate("text",  x = 20, y = 2, label = "Version B", 
           hjust = 0, size = 3.2, colour = "#9B70CC") +
  labs(
    title    = "UX Flag Prevalence: Version A vs Version B",
    subtitle = "Percentage of candidates flagged on each metric — dark = Version A, light = Version B",
    x        = "Percentage Flagged",
    y        = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title         = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#1A1A1A"),
    plot.subtitle      = element_text(colour = "#888888", size = 9),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    plot.background    = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = NA)
  )

title: AXCESSR Data Validity
text:
Score Distribution Comparison
The plots below show the distribution of domain-level scores before and after item removal. Improved item quality is expected to yield tighter, better-differentiated distributions with reduced floor and ceiling effects.


Show code
# Score Comparison Table

comparison <- validity_data %>%
  select(id,
         # Original scores
         personality_risk, values_risk,
         emotion_traits_risk, emotion_states_risk,
         total_risk_weighted,
             # Revised scores
             personality_risk_check, values_risk_check,
             emotion_traits_risk_check, emotion_states_risk_check,
             total_risk_weighted_check) %>% 
  mutate(across(everything(), as.numeric))

# Mean comparison table

comparison_table <- tibble(
  Score = c(
    "Personality Risk", "Values Risk",
    "Emotion Traits Risk", "Emotion States Risk",
    "Total Risk (Weighted)"
  ),
  Original = c(
    mean(comparison$personality_risk,        na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(comparison$values_risk,             na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(comparison$emotion_traits_risk,     na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(comparison$emotion_states_risk,     na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(comparison$total_risk_weighted,     na.rm = TRUE)
  ),
  Revised = c(
    mean(comparison$personality_risk_check,        na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(comparison$values_risk_check,             na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(comparison$emotion_traits_risk_check,     na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(comparison$emotion_states_risk_check,     na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(comparison$total_risk_weighted_check,     na.rm = TRUE)
  )
) %>%
  mutate(
    Difference  = round(Revised - Original, 4),
    Original    = round(Original, 4),
    Revised     = round(Revised, 4)
  )

kbl(comparison_table,
             col.names = c("Score", "Original", "Revised", 
                           "Difference"),
             caption   = "Mean Score Comparison: Original vs Revised") %>% 
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
    full_width = TRUE,
    font_size = 14
  )

title: AXCESSR Data Validity
text:
Reliability Comparison
Cronbach’s α was averaged across subfactors within each domain and compared between the original (V1, 247 items) and revised (V2, 206 items) instruments. Three of the four domains showed improvement following item removal, with Personality and Values showing the most meaningful gains. Emotion States improved marginally, and Emotion Traits remained stable - suggesting the removed items were contributing little noise in that domain to begin with. Taken together, these findings indicate that targeted item removal had its intended effect: a modest but consistent improvement in internal consistency without destabilising the existing structure.


Show code
# Domain Reliability Comparison

knitr::include_graphics(here::here("image/reliability_domain_dotplot.png"))








Figure 2

title: AXCESSR Data Validity
text:
PSI
To assess whether item removal materially altered candidate risk profiles, the Population Stability Index (PSI) was computed between original (V1) and revised (V2) scores at the total and domain level. PSI quantifies the distributional shift between two scoring populations and is widely used in credit risk modelling as a model monitoring metric. Scores below 0.10 indicate a stable distribution; values between 0.10 and 0.25 indicate moderate shift warranting investigation; values above 0.25 indicate significant drift requiring model review.


Show code
# PSI scores across key domains

# Total weighted score PSI
psi_total <- psi(
  base   = comparison$total_risk_weighted,
  target = comparison$total_risk_weighted_check,
  bin    = 5,
  alpha  = 0.05
)

# Domain-level PSI
psi_personality <- psi(
  base   = comparison$personality_risk,
  target = comparison$personality_risk_check,
  bin    = 5, alpha = 0.05
)

psi_values <- psi(
  base   = comparison$values_risk,
  target = comparison$values_risk_check,
  bin    = 5, alpha = 0.05
)

psi_emotion_traits <- psi(
  base   = comparison$emotion_traits_risk,
  target = comparison$emotion_traits_risk_check,
  bin    = 5, alpha = 0.05
)

psi_emotion_states <- psi(
  base   = comparison$emotion_states_risk,
  target = comparison$emotion_states_risk_check,
  bin    = 5, alpha = 0.05
)

# Pull just the PSI values cleanly
psi_table <- tibble(
  Score = c("Total Risk (Weighted)", "Personality Risk", 
            "Values Risk", "Emotion Traits Risk", "Emotion States Risk"),
  PSI = c(
    psi_total$res$psi,
    psi_personality$res$psi,
    psi_values$res$psi,
    psi_emotion_traits$res$psi,
    psi_emotion_states$res$psi
  ),
  Interpretation = c(
    "Stable - minor shift, no action required",
    "Stable - distribution largely unchanged",
    "Moderate shift - high considering only 9 items were removed",
    "Highly stable - negligible distributional change",
    "Stable - minimal shift across versions"
  )
)

kbl(
  psi_table,
  col.names = c("Score", "PSI", "Interpretation"),
  caption   = "Population Stability Index (PSI) Results"
) %>% 
  kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
    full_width = TRUE,
    font_size = 14
  )



Population Stability Index (PSI) Results


Score
PSI
Interpretation




Total Risk (Weighted)
0.0997377
Stable - minor shift, no action required


Personality Risk
0.0283380
Stable - distribution largely unchanged


Values Risk
0.1884240
Moderate shift - high considering only 9 items were removed


Emotion Traits Risk
0.0036229
Highly stable - negligible distributional change


Emotion States Risk
0.0420474
Stable - minimal shift across versions





Overall, the revised instrument demonstrated strong score stability. The total weighted risk score produced a PSI of 0.10, sitting at the boundary between stable and moderate shift — indicating that item removal had minimal practical impact on how candidates are ranked and classified overall. Emotion Traits Risk (PSI = 0.004) and Emotion States Risk (PSI = 0.042) were the most stable domains, suggesting that structural refinements in these domains preserved the underlying score distributions almost entirely.
Personality Risk showed a small but acceptable shift (PSI = 0.028), well within the stable range. Values Risk showed the largest shift of the four domains (PSI = 0.188), falling in the moderate range. This is notable given that the Values domain underwent meaningful structural consolidation — the distributional shift reflects genuine refinement of the construct rather than model instability, and remains well below the 0.25 threshold that would require intervention.
Taken together, the PSI results confirm that the revised instrument produces risk classifications that are substantively consistent with the original, while reflecting targeted improvements to construct validity at the domain level.


Show code
# PSI Bar Chart
psi_df <- tibble(
  Domain = c("Emotion Traits", "Personality", "Emotion States",
             "Total Risk\n(Weighted)", "Values"),
  PSI    = c(0.0036, 0.0283, 0.0420, 0.0997, 0.1884),
  Zone   = case_when(
    PSI < 0.10 ~ "Stable",
    PSI < 0.25 ~ "Moderate",
    TRUE       ~ "Intervention Required"
  )
) %>%
  mutate(Domain = fct_reorder(Domain, PSI))

ggplot(psi_df, aes(x = PSI, y = Domain, fill = Zone)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.55) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.10, linetype = "dashed",
             colour = "#6B3FA0", linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.25, linetype = "dashed",
             colour = "#C0392B", linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(PSI, 3)),
            hjust = -0.2, size = 3.5, colour = "#1A1A1A") +
  annotate("text", x = 0.06, y = 0.6, label = "Stable threshold",
           hjust = 0, size = 3, colour = "#6B3FA0") +
  annotate("text", x = 0.2, y = 0.6, label = "Intervention threshold",
           hjust = 0, size = 3, colour = "#C0392B") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Stable"    = "#3B1F5E",
    "Moderate"  = "#E8A838"
  )) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 0.28), expand = c(0, 0)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Population Stability Index (PSI) by Domain",
    subtitle = "V1 (247 items) vs V2 (206 items)",
    x        = "PSI",
    y        = NULL,
    fill     = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#1A1A1A"),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(colour = "#888888", size = 10),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    legend.position  = "top",
    plot.background  = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = NA)
  )  









Show code
ggsave("image/psi_bar.png", width = 12, height = 6, dpi = 180, bg = "white")


The bar chart confirms that distributional shift is concentrated in the Values domain. All other domains sit comfortably below the stable threshold, and the total weighted risk score sits precisely at the boundary (PSI = 0.10) - indicating that at the overall scoring level, V1 and V2 produce near-equivalent candidate classifications.


Show code
# Density Overlays 

score_long <- comparison %>%
  select(
    `Total Risk\n(Weighted)` = total_risk_weighted,
    `Total Risk\n(Weighted) V2` = total_risk_weighted_check,
    Personality = personality_risk,
    `Personality V2` = personality_risk_check,
    Values = values_risk,
    `Values V2` = values_risk_check,
    `Emotion Traits` = emotion_traits_risk,
    `Emotion Traits V2` = emotion_traits_risk_check,
    `Emotion States` = emotion_states_risk,
    `Emotion States V2` = emotion_states_risk_check
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "Domain", values_to = "Score") %>%
  mutate(
    Version = if_else(str_detect(Domain, "V2"), "V2 (206 items)", "V1 (247 items)"),
    Domain  = str_remove(Domain, " V2"),
    Domain  = factor(
      Domain,
      levels = c(
        "Total Risk\n(Weighted)",
        "Personality",
        "Values",
        "Emotion Traits",
        "Emotion States"
      )
    )
  )

ggplot(score_long, aes(x = Score, fill = Version, colour = Version)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.35, linewidth = 0.7) +
  facet_wrap(~ Domain, scales = "free_y", ncol = 3) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "V1 (247 items)" = "#C4A8E8",
    "V2 (206 items)" = "#3B1F5E"
  )) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(
    "V1 (247 items)" = "#9B70CC",
    "V2 (206 items)" = "#3B1F5E"
  )) +
  labs(
    title    = "Score Distributions: V1 vs V2",
    subtitle = "Density curves by domain",
    x        = "Risk Score",
    y        = "Density",
    fill     = NULL, colour = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title         = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#1A1A1A"),
    plot.subtitle      = element_text(colour = "#888888", size = 9),
    strip.text         = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#3B1F5E"),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    legend.position    = "top",
    plot.background    = element_rect(fill = "white", colour = NA)
  )









Show code
ggsave("image/psi_density.png", width = 10, height = 6, dpi = 180, bg = "white")


The density curves corroborate the PSI findings. For Emotion Traits and Personality, the V1 and V2 distributions are almost indistinguishable. Emotion States and Total Risk show a slight tightening of the distribution in V2 - consistent with the removal of noisy items reducing score variance. The Values domain shows the most visible shift, with the V2 distribution marginally narrower and shifted slightly rightward, reflecting the influence of the removed items on the lower end of the score range. Across all domains, the core shape of the distribution is preserved, confirming that the revised instrument measures the same underlying constructs in a substantively equivalent way.
